notes: Chapter argument reconstruction: 'The Time of Trouble' constructs the penultimate stage of the cosmic-conflict chain — the close of Christ's heavenly intercession (linking directly back to the investigative-judgment material of earlier chapters) and the subsequent unrestrained outbreak of persecution against Sabbath-keepers. Its argumentative weight rests entirely on (a) direct exposition of Revelation/Daniel/prophetic texts describing the close of probation, plagues, and Satan's personation of Christ, and (b) an extended typological reading of Jacob's night of wrestling (Genesis 32) as the paradigm of the saints' experience in the time of trouble, reinforced by a catalogue of other biblical deliverance narratives (Noah, Lot, Joseph, Elijah, Jeremiah, the three worthies, Daniel, Paul and Silas) offered as precedent for God's care under persecution. The one clause with a checkable historical anchor outside Scripture — Waldensian refuge in the Piedmont mountains (GC-39-068) — functions as a brief illustrative analogy, not as documentary support for a historical thesis; the chapter otherwise makes no empirical historical claims about the Reformation, papacy, or 1844 that could be independently falsified, since its subject matter is by construction future/eschatological. Verification found 0 contradicted, 0 misquotation, 0 anachronistic, 0 disputed items among 125 claims (75 supported via common-knowledge fast-path — faithful KJV renderings and faithful retellings of named Bible narratives — and 50 unverifiable theological/predictive assertions per epistemic rule 5); the defender pass had nothing in scope (0 examined, 0 defenses). Consequently this delta contains NO impact objects: there is no confirmed discrepancy (contradicted/misquotation/anachronistic) and no high-stakes disputed item to score in this chapter. Chapter-level assessment: narrative_arc = eschatological (the chapter sits entirely within the end-time crisis segment of the central thesis chain — close of probation, Sabbath/Sunday final test, plagues, Satan personating Christ, deliverance of the saints); load_bearing discrepancies = none, since there are no confirmed discrepancies to bear or not bear weight. The chapter's persuasive force rests on internal scriptural exegesis and theological narrative rather than on external historical claims, so it is structurally insulated from the kind of historiographical error that affects the Reformation-history chapters; the one quasi-historical detail present (Waldensian mountain refuge) is uncontested textbook history and carries only decorative/illustrative weight, not load-bearing argumentative weight, for this chapter's own claims about the future crisis.
